title: Infective Diarrhoea
oneLiner: Acute diarrhoea (≥3 loose stools/24h) due to infection; common but can cause severe dehydration, sepsis, and HUS—especially in the young, elderly, and immunocompromised.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hypovolaemic shock / severe dehydration:** Key finding — prolonged capillary refill (>3 s), weak/thready pulse, hypotension, oliguria. This is the immediate killer; start IV fluids before chasing a diagnosis.
- **Septic shock (bacterial translocation / toxic megacolon):** Key finding — fever + hypotension + abdominal distension / tenderness. Do NOT assume “just gastroenteritis”; check lactate, NEWS2, and start broad-spectrum antibiotics if septic.
- **Haemolytic Uraemic Syndrome (HUS) from STEC (Shiga toxin-producing E. coli):** Key finding — bloody diarrhoea + oliguria + pallor + bruising / petechiae. Check FBC, U+E, blood film for fragmented RBCs. Do NOT give antibiotics (they increase toxin release) or loperamide.
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - **Watery diarrhoea** (viral, enterotoxigenic *E. coli*, *Vibrio cholerae*, *Cryptosporidium*)
  - **Bloody diarrhoea / dysentery** (Shigella, Campylobacter, STEC, *Clostridioides difficile*, *Entamoeba histolytica*)
  - **Abdominal cramps / pain** (common with invasive pathogens)
  - **Nausea / vomiting** (more prominent in viral, *Staphylococcus aureus* food poisoning)
  - **Fever / rigors** (systemic invasion: Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia)
  - **Tenesmus** (Shigella, amoebic dysentery)
  - **Dehydration symptoms:** thirst, dizziness, reduced urine output, confusion (elderly)
- Key exam findings:
  - **Vital signs:** tachycardia, hypotension, fever, postural drop (if able to sit up)
  - **Abdominal:** diffuse tenderness without guarding (localised tenderness suggests other pathology); hyperactive bowel sounds; distension / tympany in toxic megacolon
  - **Volume status:** dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes, capillary refill >2 s
  - **Perianal:** excoriation / erythema (prolonged diarrhoea); may need proctoscopy for bleeding (rarely)
  - **Rash / signs of HUS:** petechiae, purpura, pallor; jaundice in severe infection / haemolysis
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Infective diarrhoea (viral / bacterial / parasitic)** — acute onset, travel history, food exposure, +/- fever, +/- bloody stools. Most common cause overall; usually self-limiting.
  - **Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI)** — onset after antibiotics (esp. broad‑spectrum), healthcare exposure, foul‑smelling watery diarrhoea, high WCC, elderly.
  - **Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) flare** — chronic history, extra‑intestinal manifestations (arthritis, mouth ulcers), blood + mucus, nocturnal diarrhoea. **Splitter:** no fever, symptom relapses, perianal disease / previous diagnosis.
  - **Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)** — chronic, relapsing-remitting, pain relieved by defaecation, no blood, no fever, no alarming weight loss. **Splitter:** symptom onset after a bout of gastroenteritis (post‑infectious IBS).
  - **Diverticulitis** — typically left iliac fossa pain + fever, altered bowel habit (constipation more common than diarrhoea), raised inflammatory markers. **Splitter:** localised tenderness (not diffuse), no travel history.
  - **Mesenteric ischaemia / ischaemic colitis** — elderly, AF / vascular disease, sudden onset severe pain out of proportion to tenderness, bloody diarrhoea. **Splitter:** atrial fibrillation + metabolic acidosis + lactate.
  - **Endocrine / metabolic** (thyrotoxicosis, Addison’s, VIPoma) — weight loss, flushing, prominent vomiting / diarrhoea, specific systemic findings. Rare.
- **Splitter:** **Bloody diarrhoea + recent antibiotic use + foul smell = CDI until proven otherwise.**
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - **Stool sample in red-topped container** — for microscopy, culture, sensitivity, and *C. difficile* A/B toxin (if hospital‑acquired or after antibiotics). **Must do before antibiotics** (unless septic).
  - **Urine output chart / bladder scan** — to assess dehydration.
  - **Capillary blood gas / venous blood gas** — lactate, bicarbonate (metabolic acidosis from severe dehydration / sepsis), electrolytes, glucose.
  - **NEWS2** — score guides escalation.
  - **Abdominal exam + PR exam** (if rectal bleeding, rule out local cause; also test for faecal calprotectin if IBD suspected).
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC** — WCC (elevated in bacterial / CDI), Hb (drop in bleeding / HUS), platelets (thrombocytopenia in HUS / sepsis)
  - **U+E** — AKI from dehydration; hyponatraemia / hyperkalaemia (HUS)
  - **CRP / lactate** — inflammation / sepsis; lactate >2 implies tissue hypoperfusion
  - **Blood cultures** x2 if febrile or septic
  - **Consider:** LFTs (if jaundice / suspected amoebic liver abscess), blood film (if HUS suspected — fragmented RBCs, schistocytes), HIV serology (if unexplained chronic diarrhoea / risk factors)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Abdominal X-ray (AXR) — only if severe abdominal pain, distension, or suspected toxic megacolon (colonic diameter >5.5 cm). Also to exclude perforation (free air under diaphragm).
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen / pelvis (with IV contrast) — if diagnostic uncertainty, suspected intra‑abdominal collection, IBD, or mesenteric ischaemia. **Do not delay if acute abdomen / peritonitis.**
- **Special tests:**
  - **Stool culture + sensitivity** — bacterial pathogens (Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, STEC, Yersinia, *Aeromonas*)
  - **Stool *C. difficile* toxin A+B** — mandatory in any hospital‑onset diarrhoea (≥3 loose stools/24h) or community‑onset with antibiotic exposure.
  - **Stool microscopy for ova, cysts, parasites** (Giardia, Cryptosporidium, *Entamoeba histolytica*) — if travel to endemic area, persistent diarrhoea (>7‑10 days), immunocompromised.
  - **Faecal calprotectin** — to differentiate IBD from infection (elevated in IBD); use only after stool culture negative, and with caution: infection can also elevate.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **NICE CDI severity criteria:**
    - Mild: WCC ≤15, rising creatinine <1.5x baseline
    - Moderate: WCC 15-30, creatinine 1.5-2.0x baseline
    - Severe: WCC >30, creatinine >2.0x baseline, or fever >38.5°C, or colonic distension
- **Never‑miss test:** **Stool sample for *C. difficile* toxin AND bacterial culture BEFORE starting antibiotics** (unless septic shock) — missing this means you cannot differentiate CDI from other infective diarrhoea, and you risk missing STEC, leading to inappropriate antibiotic use and HUS.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Resuscitation:** IV fluids (crystalloid: 500 mL–1 L Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline bolus) for any signs of hypovolaemia / sepsis; re‑assess.
  - **Sepsis / septic shock:** take blood cultures, give IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone 2 g IV if community‑acquired [check local policy for pathogen cover]; or piperacillin‑tazobactam if nosocomial / neutropenic). **Do NOT give antibiotics if STEC is suspected** (bloody diarrhoea + no fever + recent food exposure).
  - **Isolation:** source isolate (contact precautions) if suspected CDI (gown + gloves), or if unable to exclude highly contagious pathogen (norovirus, Shigella). Place on side room.
  - **Stool sample** — collect immediately.
  - **Notify infection control** if outbreak suspected (e.g., norovirus on ward, multiple patients with same pathogen).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Fluid balance:** monitor strict input/output chart. Continue IV fluids if unable to tolerate oral rehydration; aim for urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr. Replace ongoing losses (e.g., 200 mL additional for each loose stool).
  - **Monitor electrolytes:** check U+E daily (or more often if AKI). Correct hypokalaemia / hyponatraemia.
  - **Antibiotics (if indicated):** narrow based on stool culture sensitivities. Discontinue if cultures negative and no fever / systemic signs.
  - **Antidiarrhoeals (loperamide):** **Contraindicated** in bloody diarrhoea, fever, or suspected CDI / invasive bacterial infection — risk of toxic megacolon, HUS, prolonged illness.
  - **Consider oral zinc** (in children: improves outcome; adults: little evidence, often omitted).
  - **Diarrhoea chart + stool sample log** — record number, consistency, blood.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - For persistent diarrhoea (>7-14 days): consider stool microscopy for parasites, calprotectin, colonoscopy if IBD suspected.
  - Probiotics — limited evidence for prevention of antibiotic‑associated diarrhoea; not routinely recommended for acute infective diarrhoea in NHS.
  - If CDI confirmed: treat with oral vancomycin 125 mg QDS for 10 days (or fidaxomicin if severe/relapse). NICE CG199.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids type + rate:** IV crystalloids (Hartmann’s / 0.9% saline) at maintenance + deficit replacement; oral rehydration solution (ORS) if tolerating orally — 200–400 mL after each loose stool.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin (dose per weight/renal function) — all medical admissions with restricted mobility, regardless of diarrhoea.
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM during initial assessment if vomiting/abdominal distension; otherwise, clear fluids then light diet as tolerated. Avoid dairy (may worsen lactase deficiency post‑gastroenteritis) and high‑fibre/fat.
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 every 4 hours (more if sepsis/severe dehydration). Strict stool chart.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Oral rehydration solution (ORS):** sachet (e.g., Dioralyte) dissolved in 200 mL water. Give 200–400 mL after each loose stool; sip slowly if vomiting. Continue with breast milk or formula in children.
- **IV fluids:**
  - Resuscitation: Hartmann’s 500 mL–1 L stat over 15‑30 min (repeat to hypotension).
  - Maintenance: 0.9% saline + 5% dextrose + KCl (if K+ low) — rate per local protocol (e.g., 1 L/8h).
- **Antibiotics (empiric, community‑acquired moderate/severe):**
  - Ceftriaxone 2 g IV once daily (if non‑allergic, non‑pregnant). Duration: 3–5 days.
  - Alternative: ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BD (if severe, but avoid with known STEC or in children/pregnant — NICE NG112 restricts fluoroquinolones; [check local policy]).
- **CDI treatment (confirmed):**
  - Mild‑moderate: oral vancomycin 125 mg QDS for 10 days. If unavailable / contraindicated, oral fidaxomicin 200 mg BD for 10 days.
  - Severe: vancomycin 125–250 mg QDS (oral). If ileus: consider vancomycin enema + IV metronidazole [specialist].
  - **First recurrence:** vancomycin tapered or fidaxomicin. [Check local CDI guideline.]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Loperamide — avoid in bloody diarrhoea, fever, CDI, children <12y.
  - Antibiotics in STEC — absolute contraindication (increase risk of HUS).
  - Ciprofloxacin — risk of tendons rupture (esp. >60y, on steroids), QTc prolongation, avoid in pregnancy.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Ceftriaxone — no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment; caution in severe hepatic + renal (reduce dose).
  - Vancomycin (oral) — negligible systemic absorption; no adjustment needed for renal or hepatic.
  - Ciprofloxacin — reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g., 500 mg once daily).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving IV metronidazole alone for CDI** — it is inferior to vancomycin; oral vancomycin is the first‑line for all severities. Also: **prescribing ciprofloxacin empirically without checking for STEC** — can precipitate HUS.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Dehydration / AKI** — most common; may require IV fluids and monitoring.
  - **Electrolyte disturbances** — hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia, metabolic acidosis.
  - **Septic shock** — especially from *Salmonella*, *Shigella*, *Yersinia*.
  - **Toxic megacolon** — any severe colitis; suspect if abdominal distension, pain, fever, NEWS high; AXR shows colonic dilatation >5.5 cm.
  - **Haemolytic Uraemic Syndrome (HUS)** — from STEC (O157); triad: microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, AKI. Do not give antibiotics or loperamide.
  - **Bowel perforation / peritonitis** — from toxic megacolon or ischaemic colitis.
  - **Reactive arthritis** — after *Campylobacter*, *Salmonella*, *Shigella*, *Yersinia* (HLA‑B27 association). 2–4 weeks post‑infection.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Post‑infectious IBS** — up to 10% of patients; chronic abdominal pain, bloating, altered bowel habit after acute gastroenteritis.
  - **Lactose intolerance** — transient after infection; avoid dairy until resolved (weeks).
  - **Chronic diarrhoea (parasitic)** — *Giardia*, *Cryptosporidium* can persist in immunocompromised.
  - **Guillain‑Barré syndrome** — rare after *Campylobacter* jejuni infection (molecular mimicry). Check for ascending weakness, areflexia.
sections.Prognosis: - Overall excellent for self‑limiting viral / mild bacterial diarrhoea; symptoms resolve within 3‑7 days. Mortality from acute infective diarrhoea is low (<1% in healthy adults) but rises to 5‑10% in elderly / immunocompromised / with comorbidities. Complicated *C. difficile* has 10‑30% mortality.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always send a stool sample** before starting antibiotics (unless septic). It is the single most important test; without it, you cannot target therapy or rule out CDI/STEC.
- **Check AXR** in any patient with bloody diarrhoea, severe abdominal pain, or distension — it can diagnose toxic megacolon before any other test.
- **If bloody diarrhoea + no fever + recent travel / food history** (e.g., undercooked beef, unpasteurised dairy) → suspect STEC. Warn microbiology, initiate strict contact precautions, and **do not give antibiotics or antidiarrhoeals** — this prevents HUS.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT use loperamide** in bloody diarrhoea, fever, CDI, or suspected invasive bacterial infection — it can cause toxic megacolon and worsen HUS.
- **Do NOT start empirical antibiotics** in a well‑appearing patient with bloody diarrhoea unless you are sure it’s not STEC — antibiotics increase Shiga toxin release, precipitating HUS.
- **Do NOT forget to check for *C. difficile* ** in any patient with diarrhoea >3 days after admission or recent antibiotic use — it is the most common hospital‑acquired diarrhoea and easily missed if stool toxin not sent.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening diarrhoea (≥10 episodes/day) or new blood in stool
  - Inability to keep down fluids (vomiting, dehydration)
  - New confusion (elderly), reduced urine output (<250 mL/12h), or rapid breathing
  - Severe abdominal pain, distension, or fever >38°C
- **Follow‑up:** Only required if symptoms persist >7–10 days (consider stool microscopy for parasites, faecal calprotectin, IBD workup) or if immunocompromised. Otherwise, no routine follow‑up.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior (registrar / consultant):**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L after initial fluid replacement
  - GCS <15 or new confusion (elderly)
  - Abdominal distension / tenderness with fever → suspect toxic megacolon
  - Bloody diarrhoea + oliguria + falling platelets / Hb → HUS
  - Failure to respond to initial fluids (persistent tachycardia, hypotension)
- **When to call ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Need for vasopressors or invasive ventilation
  - Peritonitis / suspected perforation (immediate surgical review)

rawMarkdown:
## Infective Diarrhoea
**One-liner:** Acute diarrhoea (≥3 loose stools/24h) due to infection; common but can cause severe dehydration, sepsis, and HUS—especially in the young, elderly, and immunocompromised.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Hypovolaemic shock / severe dehydration:** Key finding — prolonged capillary refill (>3 s), weak/thready pulse, hypotension, oliguria. This is the immediate killer; start IV fluids before chasing a diagnosis.
- **Septic shock (bacterial translocation / toxic megacolon):** Key finding — fever + hypotension + abdominal distension / tenderness. Do NOT assume “just gastroenteritis”; check lactate, NEWS2, and start broad-spectrum antibiotics if septic.
- **Haemolytic Uraemic Syndrome (HUS) from STEC (Shiga toxin-producing E. coli):** Key finding — bloody diarrhoea + oliguria + pallor + bruising / petechiae. Check FBC, U+E, blood film for fragmented RBCs. Do NOT give antibiotics (they increase toxin release) or loperamide.

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - **Watery diarrhoea** (viral, enterotoxigenic *E. coli*, *Vibrio cholerae*, *Cryptosporidium*)
  - **Bloody diarrhoea / dysentery** (Shigella, Campylobacter, STEC, *Clostridioides difficile*, *Entamoeba histolytica*)
  - **Abdominal cramps / pain** (common with invasive pathogens)
  - **Nausea / vomiting** (more prominent in viral, *Staphylococcus aureus* food poisoning)
  - **Fever / rigors** (systemic invasion: Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia)
  - **Tenesmus** (Shigella, amoebic dysentery)
  - **Dehydration symptoms:** thirst, dizziness, reduced urine output, confusion (elderly)
- Key exam findings:
  - **Vital signs:** tachycardia, hypotension, fever, postural drop (if able to sit up)
  - **Abdominal:** diffuse tenderness without guarding (localised tenderness suggests other pathology); hyperactive bowel sounds; distension / tympany in toxic megacolon
  - **Volume status:** dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes, capillary refill >2 s
  - **Perianal:** excoriation / erythema (prolonged diarrhoea); may need proctoscopy for bleeding (rarely)
  - **Rash / signs of HUS:** petechiae, purpura, pallor; jaundice in severe infection / haemolysis

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Infective diarrhoea (viral / bacterial / parasitic)** — acute onset, travel history, food exposure, +/- fever, +/- bloody stools. Most common cause overall; usually self-limiting.
  - **Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI)** — onset after antibiotics (esp. broad‑spectrum), healthcare exposure, foul‑smelling watery diarrhoea, high WCC, elderly.
  - **Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) flare** — chronic history, extra‑intestinal manifestations (arthritis, mouth ulcers), blood + mucus, nocturnal diarrhoea. **Splitter:** no fever, symptom relapses, perianal disease / previous diagnosis.
  - **Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)** — chronic, relapsing-remitting, pain relieved by defaecation, no blood, no fever, no alarming weight loss. **Splitter:** symptom onset after a bout of gastroenteritis (post‑infectious IBS).
  - **Diverticulitis** — typically left iliac fossa pain + fever, altered bowel habit (constipation more common than diarrhoea), raised inflammatory markers. **Splitter:** localised tenderness (not diffuse), no travel history.
  - **Mesenteric ischaemia / ischaemic colitis** — elderly, AF / vascular disease, sudden onset severe pain out of proportion to tenderness, bloody diarrhoea. **Splitter:** atrial fibrillation + metabolic acidosis + lactate.
  - **Endocrine / metabolic** (thyrotoxicosis, Addison’s, VIPoma) — weight loss, flushing, prominent vomiting / diarrhoea, specific systemic findings. Rare.
- **Splitter:** **Bloody diarrhoea + recent antibiotic use + foul smell = CDI until proven otherwise.**

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - **Stool sample in red-topped container** — for microscopy, culture, sensitivity, and *C. difficile* A/B toxin (if hospital‑acquired or after antibiotics). **Must do before antibiotics** (unless septic).
  - **Urine output chart / bladder scan** — to assess dehydration.
  - **Capillary blood gas / venous blood gas** — lactate, bicarbonate (metabolic acidosis from severe dehydration / sepsis), electrolytes, glucose.
  - **NEWS2** — score guides escalation.
  - **Abdominal exam + PR exam** (if rectal bleeding, rule out local cause; also test for faecal calprotectin if IBD suspected).
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC** — WCC (elevated in bacterial / CDI), Hb (drop in bleeding / HUS), platelets (thrombocytopenia in HUS / sepsis)
  - **U+E** — AKI from dehydration; hyponatraemia / hyperkalaemia (HUS)
  - **CRP / lactate** — inflammation / sepsis; lactate >2 implies tissue hypoperfusion
  - **Blood cultures** x2 if febrile or septic
  - **Consider:** LFTs (if jaundice / suspected amoebic liver abscess), blood film (if HUS suspected — fragmented RBCs, schistocytes), HIV serology (if unexplained chronic diarrhoea / risk factors)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Abdominal X-ray (AXR) — only if severe abdominal pain, distension, or suspected toxic megacolon (colonic diameter >5.5 cm). Also to exclude perforation (free air under diaphragm).
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen / pelvis (with IV contrast) — if diagnostic uncertainty, suspected intra‑abdominal collection, IBD, or mesenteric ischaemia. **Do not delay if acute abdomen / peritonitis.**
- **Special tests:**
  - **Stool culture + sensitivity** — bacterial pathogens (Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, STEC, Yersinia, *Aeromonas*)
  - **Stool *C. difficile* toxin A+B** — mandatory in any hospital‑onset diarrhoea (≥3 loose stools/24h) or community‑onset with antibiotic exposure.
  - **Stool microscopy for ova, cysts, parasites** (Giardia, Cryptosporidium, *Entamoeba histolytica*) — if travel to endemic area, persistent diarrhoea (>7‑10 days), immunocompromised.
  - **Faecal calprotectin** — to differentiate IBD from infection (elevated in IBD); use only after stool culture negative, and with caution: infection can also elevate.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **NICE CDI severity criteria:**
    - Mild: WCC ≤15, rising creatinine <1.5x baseline
    - Moderate: WCC 15-30, creatinine 1.5-2.0x baseline
    - Severe: WCC >30, creatinine >2.0x baseline, or fever >38.5°C, or colonic distension
- **Never‑miss test:** **Stool sample for *C. difficile* toxin AND bacterial culture BEFORE starting antibiotics** (unless septic shock) — missing this means you cannot differentiate CDI from other infective diarrhoea, and you risk missing STEC, leading to inappropriate antibiotic use and HUS.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Resuscitation:** IV fluids (crystalloid: 500 mL–1 L Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline bolus) for any signs of hypovolaemia / sepsis; re‑assess.
  - **Sepsis / septic shock:** take blood cultures, give IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone 2 g IV if community‑acquired [check local policy for pathogen cover]; or piperacillin‑tazobactam if nosocomial / neutropenic). **Do NOT give antibiotics if STEC is suspected** (bloody diarrhoea + no fever + recent food exposure).
  - **Isolation:** source isolate (contact precautions) if suspected CDI (gown + gloves), or if unable to exclude highly contagious pathogen (norovirus, Shigella). Place on side room.
  - **Stool sample** — collect immediately.
  - **Notify infection control** if outbreak suspected (e.g., norovirus on ward, multiple patients with same pathogen).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Fluid balance:** monitor strict input/output chart. Continue IV fluids if unable to tolerate oral rehydration; aim for urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr. Replace ongoing losses (e.g., 200 mL additional for each loose stool).
  - **Monitor electrolytes:** check U+E daily (or more often if AKI). Correct hypokalaemia / hyponatraemia.
  - **Antibiotics (if indicated):** narrow based on stool culture sensitivities. Discontinue if cultures negative and no fever / systemic signs.
  - **Antidiarrhoeals (loperamide):** **Contraindicated** in bloody diarrhoea, fever, or suspected CDI / invasive bacterial infection — risk of toxic megacolon, HUS, prolonged illness.
  - **Consider oral zinc** (in children: improves outcome; adults: little evidence, often omitted).
  - **Diarrhoea chart + stool sample log** — record number, consistency, blood.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - For persistent diarrhoea (>7-14 days): consider stool microscopy for parasites, calprotectin, colonoscopy if IBD suspected.
  - Probiotics — limited evidence for prevention of antibiotic‑associated diarrhoea; not routinely recommended for acute infective diarrhoea in NHS.
  - If CDI confirmed: treat with oral vancomycin 125 mg QDS for 10 days (or fidaxomicin if severe/relapse). NICE CG199.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids type + rate:** IV crystalloids (Hartmann’s / 0.9% saline) at maintenance + deficit replacement; oral rehydration solution (ORS) if tolerating orally — 200–400 mL after each loose stool.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin (dose per weight/renal function) — all medical admissions with restricted mobility, regardless of diarrhoea.
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM during initial assessment if vomiting/abdominal distension; otherwise, clear fluids then light diet as tolerated. Avoid dairy (may worsen lactase deficiency post‑gastroenteritis) and high‑fibre/fat.
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 every 4 hours (more if sepsis/severe dehydration). Strict stool chart.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Oral rehydration solution (ORS):** sachet (e.g., Dioralyte) dissolved in 200 mL water. Give 200–400 mL after each loose stool; sip slowly if vomiting. Continue with breast milk or formula in children.
- **IV fluids:**
  - Resuscitation: Hartmann’s 500 mL–1 L stat over 15‑30 min (repeat to hypotension).
  - Maintenance: 0.9% saline + 5% dextrose + KCl (if K+ low) — rate per local protocol (e.g., 1 L/8h).
- **Antibiotics (empiric, community‑acquired moderate/severe):**
  - Ceftriaxone 2 g IV once daily (if non‑allergic, non‑pregnant). Duration: 3–5 days.
  - Alternative: ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BD (if severe, but avoid with known STEC or in children/pregnant — NICE NG112 restricts fluoroquinolones; [check local policy]).
- **CDI treatment (confirmed):**
  - Mild‑moderate: oral vancomycin 125 mg QDS for 10 days. If unavailable / contraindicated, oral fidaxomicin 200 mg BD for 10 days.
  - Severe: vancomycin 125–250 mg QDS (oral). If ileus: consider vancomycin enema + IV metronidazole [specialist].
  - **First recurrence:** vancomycin tapered or fidaxomicin. [Check local CDI guideline.]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Loperamide — avoid in bloody diarrhoea, fever, CDI, children <12y.
  - Antibiotics in STEC — absolute contraindication (increase risk of HUS).
  - Ciprofloxacin — risk of tendons rupture (esp. >60y, on steroids), QTc prolongation, avoid in pregnancy.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Ceftriaxone — no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment; caution in severe hepatic + renal (reduce dose).
  - Vancomycin (oral) — negligible systemic absorption; no adjustment needed for renal or hepatic.
  - Ciprofloxacin — reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g., 500 mg once daily).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving IV metronidazole alone for CDI** — it is inferior to vancomycin; oral vancomycin is the first‑line for all severities. Also: **prescribing ciprofloxacin empirically without checking for STEC** — can precipitate HUS.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Dehydration / AKI** — most common; may require IV fluids and monitoring.
  - **Electrolyte disturbances** — hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia, metabolic acidosis.
  - **Septic shock** — especially from *Salmonella*, *Shigella*, *Yersinia*.
  - **Toxic megacolon** — any severe colitis; suspect if abdominal distension, pain, fever, NEWS high; AXR shows colonic dilatation >5.5 cm.
  - **Haemolytic Uraemic Syndrome (HUS)** — from STEC (O157); triad: microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, AKI. Do not give antibiotics or loperamide.
  - **Bowel perforation / peritonitis** — from toxic megacolon or ischaemic colitis.
  - **Reactive arthritis** — after *Campylobacter*, *Salmonella*, *Shigella*, *Yersinia* (HLA‑B27 association). 2–4 weeks post‑infection.
- **Chronic:**
  - **Post‑infectious IBS** — up to 10% of patients; chronic abdominal pain, bloating, altered bowel habit after acute gastroenteritis.
  - **Lactose intolerance** — transient after infection; avoid dairy until resolved (weeks).
  - **Chronic diarrhoea (parasitic)** — *Giardia*, *Cryptosporidium* can persist in immunocompromised.
  - **Guillain‑Barré syndrome** — rare after *Campylobacter* jejuni infection (molecular mimicry). Check for ascending weakness, areflexia.

### Prognosis
- Overall excellent for self‑limiting viral / mild bacterial diarrhoea; symptoms resolve within 3‑7 days. Mortality from acute infective diarrhoea is low (<1% in healthy adults) but rises to 5‑10% in elderly / immunocompromised / with comorbidities. Complicated *C. difficile* has 10‑30% mortality.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always send a stool sample** before starting antibiotics (unless septic). It is the single most important test; without it, you cannot target therapy or rule out CDI/STEC.
- **Check AXR** in any patient with bloody diarrhoea, severe abdominal pain, or distension — it can diagnose toxic megacolon before any other test.
- **If bloody diarrhoea + no fever + recent travel / food history** (e.g., undercooked beef, unpasteurised dairy) → suspect STEC. Warn microbiology, initiate strict contact precautions, and **do not give antibiotics or antidiarrhoeals** — this prevents HUS.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT use loperamide** in bloody diarrhoea, fever, CDI, or suspected invasive bacterial infection — it can cause toxic megacolon and worsen HUS.
- **Do NOT start empirical antibiotics** in a well‑appearing patient with bloody diarrhoea unless you are sure it’s not STEC — antibiotics increase Shiga toxin release, precipitating HUS.
- **Do NOT forget to check for *C. difficile* ** in any patient with diarrhoea >3 days after admission or recent antibiotic use — it is the most common hospital‑acquired diarrhoea and easily missed if stool toxin not sent.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening diarrhoea (≥10 episodes/day) or new blood in stool
  - Inability to keep down fluids (vomiting, dehydration)
  - New confusion (elderly), reduced urine output (<250 mL/12h), or rapid breathing
  - Severe abdominal pain, distension, or fever >38°C
- **Follow‑up:** Only required if symptoms persist >7–10 days (consider stool microscopy for parasites, faecal calprotectin, IBD workup) or if immunocompromised. Otherwise, no routine follow‑up.

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior (registrar / consultant):**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L after initial fluid replacement
  - GCS <15 or new confusion (elderly)
  - Abdominal distension / tenderness with fever → suspect toxic megacolon
  - Bloody diarrhoea + oliguria + falling platelets / Hb → HUS
  - Failure to respond to initial fluids (persistent tachycardia, hypotension)
- **When to call ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Need for vasopressors or invasive ventilation
  - Peritonitis / suspected perforation (immediate surgical review)
